g.data-narration: Welcome to Contiguous Memory Allocation - a fundamental memory management technique in operating systems.
In contiguous allocation, each process occupies a single continuous block of physical memory, enabling fast sequential access.
g.text: A memory management technique where each process is allocated
a SINGLE contiguous block of physical memory.

g.data-narration: The OS maintains a memory map tracking occupied partitions and free holes using data structures like linked lists or bitmaps.
g.text: Memory Management: Linked Lists | Bitmaps | Partition Tables

g.data-narration: Fixed partitioning divides memory into predetermined partition sizes. Processes are assigned to the smallest available partition that fits them.
g.text: Memory is divided into FIXED-SIZE partitions during system initialization.
Common in early batch processing systems (e.g., IBM OS/360).
⚠ Internal Fragmentation occurs when process size < partition size

g.data-narration: Let's calculate internal fragmentation: P1 gets 4MB (1MB wasted), P2 gets 8MB (3MB wasted), P3 gets 4MB (2MB wasted) - total 6MB wasted.
Dynamic partitioning creates partitions based on actual process requirements, reducing internal fragmentation but causing external fragmentation.
g.text: Partitions are created DYNAMICALLY based on process requirements.
Eliminates internal fragmentation but causes EXTERNAL fragmentation over time.

g.data-narration: First-Fit allocates memory to the first hole that can accommodate the process. It's fast but may create uneven holes throughout memory.
Best-Fit searches all holes and allocates the smallest one that fits. This minimizes leftover space but leaves tiny unusable fragments.
Worst-Fit allocates to the largest available hole. This leaves larger remaining blocks but quickly eliminates large memory holes.
Compare strategies: First-Fit is fastest but uneven; Best-Fit minimizes waste but causes fragmentation; Worst-Fit leaves large blocks but wastes memory.
Internal fragmentation occurs within allocated partitions due to fixed block sizes being larger than requested - this is wasted space.
External fragmentation occurs when total free memory exists but is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks - cannot satisfy large requests.
g.text: 50% Rule: N blocks → N/2 lost to fragmentation

g.data-narration: Compaction consolidates free memory by moving all allocated partitions together, creating one large contiguous free block.
Contiguous allocation is used in embedded systems, database systems, boot loaders, and early operating systems like DOS.
